title: Knowledge Check: Whiskey
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 10, geography, social studies, economics, and language, plus the bonus science lesson on proof.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the bigger questions, how knowledge travels and who deserves credit for it, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What route did distillation knowledge travel before reaching whiskey?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"From Arab science through European monasteries to Ireland, Scotland, America, and Japan"},{"id":"q1b","text":"It was invented in Kentucky"},{"id":"q1c","text":"It came from ancient Egypt only"},{"id":"q1d","text":"It has no history"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Distillation was perfected as Arab science around the 8th century, passed through European monasteries, reached Ireland and Scotland by the 1400s, then America and Japan. Technology follows trade routes.","citation":"Lesson: The Geography of Distillation"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Why is karst limestone water ideal for making bourbon?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"It is rich in calcium and magnesium and free of iron"},{"id":"q2b","text":"It is full of iron"},{"id":"q2c","text":"It is salty"},{"id":"q2d","text":"It has no minerals at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Karst water carries calcium and magnesium that feed the yeast, and the limestone filters out iron, which would otherwise give whiskey a metallic taste and dark color.","citation":"Lesson: Karst Limestone and Bourbon"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"Which U.S. state is a hidden giant of bourbon production?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Indiana, especially Lawrenceburg, which sits on the karst formation"},{"id":"q3b","text":"Florida"},{"id":"q3c","text":"Maine"},{"id":"q3d","text":"Arizona"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Lawrenceburg, Indiana sits on the same karst limestone as Kentucky, and a distillery there has produced spirits since the 1840s, so much bourbon is actually made in Indiana.","citation":"Lesson: Karst Limestone and Bourbon"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"How does climate make Kentucky bourbon age faster than Scotch whisky?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"Big seasonal temperature swings push whiskey in and out of the wood faster"},{"id":"q4b","text":"Kentucky barrels are bigger"},{"id":"q4c","text":"Scotland uses no barrels"},{"id":"q4d","text":"Bourbon is heated on a stove"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Kentucky's continental climate swings from hot summers to cold winters, driving the whiskey into and out of the charred oak quickly, while Scotland's mild climate barely swings.","citation":"Lesson: The Geography of Aging"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"What is the angel's share?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"The 2 to 4 percent of whiskey that evaporates from the barrel each year"},{"id":"q5b","text":"A tax on whiskey"},{"id":"q5c","text":"The best barrel in a batch"},{"id":"q5d","text":"A type of yeast"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Each year about 2 to 4 percent of the whiskey simply evaporates from the barrel, a real and costly loss distillers call the angel's share.","citation":"Lesson: The Geography of Aging"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"Why did the same Scottish barley make smoky Islay whisky and sweet Speyside whisky?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Different local geography, like seaweed peat versus a granite valley, shapes the flavor"},{"id":"q6b","text":"They use different grains"},{"id":"q6c","text":"One is not really whisky"},{"id":"q6d","text":"The barley is dyed"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Islay's seaweed-rich peat gives a smoky, briny dram, while Speyside's sheltered granite valley gives a sweet, fruity one, the same terroir idea from beer and wine.","citation":"Lesson: Karst Limestone and Bourbon"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"Why did Hamilton's 1791 whiskey tax fall hardest on frontier farmers?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"Whiskey was their currency and they had no cash to pay with, while large eastern distillers absorbed the cost"},{"id":"q7b","text":"They drank more than easterners"},{"id":"q7c","text":"They lived closer to the capital"},{"id":"q7d","text":"They paid no other taxes"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"On the frontier whiskey served as money, so taxing it taxed their entire monetary system, while big eastern distillers could spread the cost over large production.","citation":"Lesson: From Rebellion to Republic"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"What precedent did the Whiskey Rebellion of 1794 establish?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"That the federal government could enforce its taxes with military force"},{"id":"q8b","text":"That taxes are always illegal"},{"id":"q8c","text":"That states could ignore federal law"},{"id":"q8d","text":"That whiskey was banned"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Washington led about 13,000 troops against the rebels, the only sitting president to lead troops in the field, setting the precedent that federal taxes could be enforced by force.","citation":"Lesson: From Rebellion to Republic"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"Who was Nathan Green?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"An enslaved master distiller in Tennessee who taught Jack Daniel"},{"id":"q9b","text":"A French winemaker"},{"id":"q9c","text":"A Scottish friar"},{"id":"q9d","text":"A modern brand executive"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Nathan 'Nearest' Green was an enslaved man in Lincoln County, Tennessee, the master distiller who taught Jack Daniel, a role left out of the brand's story until 2016.","citation":"Lesson: The Man Who Taught Jack"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What is the Lincoln County Process?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"Filtering new whiskey through about 10 feet of sugar maple charcoal, the legal mark of Tennessee whiskey"},{"id":"q10b","text":"A type of barrel"},{"id":"q10c","text":"A federal tax law"},{"id":"q10d","text":"A grape-growing method"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"The Lincoln County Process drips new whiskey through sugar maple charcoal before barreling, which legally distinguishes Tennessee whiskey from bourbon.","citation":"Lesson: The Man Who Taught Jack"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"Why did Nathan Green's contribution disappear from whiskey history for 130 years?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Structural forces: Jim Crow hierarchy, single-founder branding, archives that recorded enslaved people as property, and no one asking"},{"id":"q11b","text":"He asked to be left out"},{"id":"q11c","text":"He never existed"},{"id":"q11d","text":"A single law erased him"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"His erasure was structural, not one act: racial hierarchy, simplified single-founder marketing, archives that named enslaved people as property, and the absence of inquiry until 2016.","citation":"Lesson: The Man Who Taught Jack"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"What is the difference between documented and historically probable claims about Nathan Green?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"Documented facts are confirmed by records; probable claims, like his exact teaching role, rest on corroborated oral history"},{"id":"q12b","text":"There is no difference"},{"id":"q12c","text":"Probable claims are always false"},{"id":"q12d","text":"Documented claims are guesses"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"Responsible history separates what records confirm (Green was enslaved there, his family later worked at the distillery) from what oral history makes probable (that he was the master teacher).","citation":"Lesson: The Man Who Taught Jack"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"How did Masataka Taketsuru challenge the idea that great whisky needed Scottish heritage?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"He deliberately learned Scotch production in Scotland and built award-winning whisky in Japan"},{"id":"q13b","text":"He banned Scotch in Japan"},{"id":"q13c","text":"He proved only Scotland could make whisky"},{"id":"q13d","text":"He never left Japan"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Taketsuru studied in Scotland from 1918, then chose a Japanese site that mimicked Scottish climate, showing craft knowledge can be learned and carried anywhere. Excellence has no passport.","citation":"Lesson: Whisky Crosses the Ocean"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"Roughly how large is the global whisky market?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"About 87 billion dollars a year across all categories"},{"id":"q14b","text":"About 1 million dollars"},{"id":"q14c","text":"There is no global market"},{"id":"q14d","text":"About 100 dollars"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"The global whisky market is about 87 billion dollars a year, with Scotch exports near 6 billion and American whiskey near 4 billion and growing fast.","citation":"Lesson: The Money in the Barrel"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"Why does a 12-year bourbon cost more to produce than a 4-year bourbon?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"Extra years add storage cost, more angel's-share evaporation, and the capital cost of money tied up"},{"id":"q15b","text":"Older whiskey uses gold barrels"},{"id":"q15c","text":"It does not cost more"},{"id":"q15d","text":"The grain is more expensive"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Each year locked in the barrel adds storage, more evaporation loss, and the opportunity cost of capital, so a 12-year bourbon costs about three times as much to produce before any brand premium.","citation":"Lesson: The Money in the Barrel"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"What does the MGP case in Lawrenceburg, Indiana reveal about whiskey branding?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"The same bulk liquid can be rebottled as a pricier craft brand on the strength of a story"},{"id":"q16b","text":"Indiana makes no whiskey"},{"id":"q16c","text":"All brands distill their own whiskey"},{"id":"q16d","text":"Brand stories never affect price"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"MGP sells bulk whiskey to many brands, so a 20 to 30 dollar house liquid can become a 60 to 100 dollar craft brand, raising the line between marketing and misleading.","citation":"Lesson: The Money in the Barrel"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What makes Uncle Nearest's premium pricing different from a typical craft brand?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"It rests on a documented, recovered historical record rather than a constructed story"},{"id":"q17b","text":"It charges less than all other brands"},{"id":"q17c","text":"It hides who it is named for"},{"id":"q17d","text":"It uses no marketing"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Most craft brands build a constructed heritage story, but Uncle Nearest's premium rests on verifiable history, showing the market rewards accurate authenticity.","citation":"Lesson: The Economics of Attribution"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What did Uncle Nearest achieve as a business?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"It raised more than 200 million dollars and became the top-selling Black-owned spirits brand in the U.S."},{"id":"q18b","text":"It went out of business in a year"},{"id":"q18c","text":"It only sold in one town"},{"id":"q18d","text":"It made no money"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Founded in 2017 by Fawn Weaver and named for Nathan Green, Uncle Nearest raised over 200 million dollars by 2022 and became the top-selling Black-owned spirits brand.","citation":"Lesson: The Economics of Attribution"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"How does the Nearest Green Scholarship Fund change the brand's economic model?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"It directs proceeds to Black higher education, making the model reparative rather than just symbolic"},{"id":"q19b","text":"It raises prices for no reason"},{"id":"q19c","text":"It has no real effect"},{"id":"q19d","text":"It pays only executives"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"By committing proceeds to Black higher education, the scholarship fund makes the recovery of Green's story a structural economic act, not only an acknowledgment.","citation":"Lesson: The Economics of Attribution"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"In the United States, how is proof related to alcohol by volume?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"Proof is the alcohol percentage times two, so 40 percent is 80 proof"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Proof equals the alcohol percentage exactly"},{"id":"q20c","text":"Proof is the alcohol percentage divided by two"},{"id":"q20d","text":"Proof has nothing to do with alcohol"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"United States proof is simply twice the alcohol percentage, a doubling adopted in 1848 to make excise tax math easy.","citation":"Lesson: What Does Proof Mean?"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"Where did the word proof come from?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"A naval gunpowder test: if spirit mixed with gunpowder burned, it was proved strong"},{"id":"q21b","text":"A brand of whiskey"},{"id":"q21c","text":"A French wine law"},{"id":"q21d","text":"The name of a distiller"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"From the Latin probare, to test, sailors proved a spirit by mixing it with gunpowder and lighting it; the threshold where it reliably ignites is about 57 percent alcohol.","citation":"Lesson: What Does Proof Mean?"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"How does a hydrometer measure alcohol content?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"It floats at different heights because alcohol is less dense than water"},{"id":"q22b","text":"It measures the color"},{"id":"q22c","text":"It tastes the spirit"},{"id":"q22d","text":"It weighs the bottle"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Ethanol is lighter than water, so a weighted float sinks lower in higher-alcohol liquid; its scale reads the alcohol percentage, corrected for temperature.","citation":"Lesson: What Does Proof Mean?"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"Why does proof affect how much tax a barrel of whiskey owes?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Tax is charged per proof gallon, so higher-proof whiskey owes more, and adding water lowers the tax"},{"id":"q23b","text":"Proof has no effect on tax"},{"id":"q23c","text":"Lower proof always pays more"},{"id":"q23d","text":"Only color is taxed"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"A proof gallon is one gallon at 100 proof, so a stronger barrel holds more proof gallons and owes more tax, which is why distillers often add water before bottling.","citation":"Lesson: What Does Proof Mean?"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What do the Arabic roots of 'alcohol' (al-kuhl) and 'alembic' (al-ambiq) reveal?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"The Islamic Golden Age's central role in developing distillation"},{"id":"q24b","text":"That whiskey began in Ireland"},{"id":"q24c","text":"That the words are meaningless"},{"id":"q24d","text":"That distillation is modern"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"The Arabic origins of alcohol and alembic preserve, in language, the Islamic Golden Age's role in developing the science of distillation.","citation":"Lesson: The Water of Life"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What does the word whiskey, from uisce beatha, literally mean?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"Water of life, the same meaning as the Latin aqua vitae"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Fire and smoke"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Golden grain"},{"id":"q25d","text":"Strong drink"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Whiskey comes from the Gaelic uisce beatha, water of life, the same phrase as the Latin aqua vitae, showing two cultures naming the spirit identically.","citation":"Lesson: The Water of Life"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"Why is etymology described as historical evidence in this episode?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"The languages preserved in whiskey's words record which cultures carried the technology"},{"id":"q26b","text":"Words are chosen at random"},{"id":"q26c","text":"Etymology proves nothing"},{"id":"q26d","text":"Only English matters"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Tracing words back to Arabic, Gaelic, and Latin maps the cultural journey of distillation, so the vocabulary itself is a kind of historical record.","citation":"Lesson: The Water of Life"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"Why is oral history considered a legitimate scholarly method?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"It is a peer-reviewed method with professional guidelines, used to recover knowledge that written records excluded"},{"id":"q27b","text":"It is just gossip"},{"id":"q27c","text":"It replaces all written records"},{"id":"q27d","text":"It is never cited by scholars"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Oral history is the systematic, critical collection of personal and community narratives, with guidelines from the Oral History Association (founded 1966), and is cited alongside written sources.","citation":"Lesson: The Oral History That Survived"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"How did oral history preserve Nathan Green's story?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"His descendants kept his memory in family tradition for 130 years, which guided a journalist to corroborating records"},{"id":"q28b","text":"It was written in the company history"},{"id":"q28c","text":"It was never preserved"},{"id":"q28d","text":"A government archive saved it"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Green's family kept his story alive for 130 years; when Clay Risen sought written corroboration he found enough to publish, so oral history told the investigator where to look.","citation":"Lesson: The Oral History That Survived"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"When is oral history especially important?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"When written records were never created, were destroyed, or excluded certain people"},{"id":"q29b","text":"Only when written records are plentiful"},{"id":"q29c","text":"Never"},{"id":"q29d","text":"Only for famous people"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Oral history matters most when written records were absent, destroyed, or systematically excluded people, as with enslaved workers recorded as property rather than knowledge holders.","citation":"Lesson: The Oral History That Survived"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What is the running theme of the whiskey episode?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"Excellence has no passport: knowledge and skill can travel and be learned anywhere"},{"id":"q30b","text":"Only Scotland makes good whisky"},{"id":"q30c","text":"Whiskey is a modern invention"},{"id":"q30d","text":"Geography does not matter"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"From Arab science to Japanese whisky to the recovery of Nathan Green's story, the episode shows that excellence and knowledge are not bound to one place or people.","citation":"Lesson: Fire Water (intro)"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"Why must bourbon age in new charred oak barrels?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"The charred wood gives vanilla, caramel, and color as the whiskey moves in and out with the seasons"},{"id":"q31b","text":"To make it cheaper"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Because plastic is illegal"},{"id":"q31d","text":"It does not need barrels"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Seasonal temperature swings drive the whiskey into the charred oak, where it extracts vanilla, caramel, tannins, and color, then back out to integrate them.","citation":"Lesson: The Geography of Aging"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What kind of human achievement is distillation, according to the episode?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"A technology, a piece of knowledge that can be learned and carried anywhere"},{"id":"q32b","text":"A natural plant"},{"id":"q32c","text":"A type of soil"},{"id":"q32d","text":"A weather pattern"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Unlike beer's recipe or wine's place, whiskey rests on distillation, a technology that humans carried across 1,300 years and six continents.","citation":"Lesson: Fire Water (intro)"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What did the 2016 New York Times investigation by Clay Risen accomplish?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"It documented and publicized Nathan Green's role, prompting the distillery to acknowledge him"},{"id":"q33b","text":"It invented the Lincoln County Process"},{"id":"q33c","text":"It closed the distillery"},{"id":"q33d","text":"It had no effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Risen's 2016 article brought Green's documented and corroborated story to a wide public, and the distillery publicly acknowledged his contribution that year.","citation":"Lesson: The Man Who Taught Jack"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What does it mean that whiskey aging is illiquid?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"The product is locked in barrels for years before it can be sold, tying up capital"},{"id":"q34b","text":"The whiskey is solid"},{"id":"q34c","text":"It cannot be poured"},{"id":"q34d","text":"It is sold instantly"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Aged whiskey is literally and financially illiquid: it sits in barrels for years, so producers carry the cost of capital tied up the whole time.","citation":"Lesson: The Money in the Barrel"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"Which Scottish region is known for heavily smoked, briny whisky?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"Islay, with its seaweed-rich peat bogs"},{"id":"q35b","text":"Speyside, in a sheltered valley"},{"id":"q35c","text":"The Lowlands"},{"id":"q35d","text":"Kentucky"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"Islay's maritime peat, full of seaweed, gives its whisky a heavily smoked, briny character impossible to make in inland Speyside.","citation":"Lesson: Karst Limestone and Bourbon"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"Why did Taketsuru choose Yoichi on Hokkaido for his distillery?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Its cool maritime climate, water, and snowfall mimicked Scottish conditions"},{"id":"q36b","text":"It was the cheapest land"},{"id":"q36c","text":"It was near Tokyo"},{"id":"q36d","text":"He inherited it"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Taketsuru deliberately chose Yoichi because its cool coastal climate and clean water approximated Scotland, showing geography matters as much as technique.","citation":"Lesson: Whisky Crosses the Ocean"}]}
